primary_diagnosis.name: Ischaemic heart disease presenting as new-onset crescendo angina, suspected unstable angina within the acute coronary syndrome spectrum

pathophysiology_summary: Atherosclerotic narrowing of one or more coronary arteries limits myocardial oxygen supply. On exertion, rising myocardial oxygen demand outstrips the fixed/narrowed supply, producing transient demand ischaemia felt as exertional retrosternal tightness with radiation to the jaw and left arm, relieved by rest as demand falls. The NEW onset and the accelerating (crescendo) pattern over 48 hours signal an unstable, possibly plaque-rupture-related process — i.e. unstable angina, which sits on the acute coronary syndrome spectrum alongside NSTEMI/STEMI and carries a real short-term risk of progression to myocardial infarction. Modifiable risk factors (current smoking, hyperlipidaemia with poor statin adherence) raise the pre-test probability of coronary disease. The auscultated S4 gallop reflects reduced left-ventricular compliance from ischaemia. Definitive risk stratification requires a resting 12-lead ECG, serial troponin, and downstream functional/anatomical imaging — none obtainable via telehealth.

differential.evidence_basis: Exertional, central, squeezing chest tightness radiating to jaw/arm and relieved by rest is a textbook ischaemic pattern.
Retrosternal discomfort can be refluxrelated, but reflux is typically post-prandial or on lying down, not strictly exertional.
Common benign mimic; would usually be reproducible on palpation or altered by movement/breathing.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Reflux and chest wall pain are legitimate to consider but must not be used to downgrade an exertional, radiating, risk-factor-laden pain before ischaemia is excluded.

differential.evidence_basis: New-onset pain accelerating over 48 hours with radiation, nausea and exertional dyspnoea in a smoker with hyperlipidaemia.
Effort-related, rest-relieved pain fits angina — but the crescendo, new-onset course makes 'stable' unsafe as the working label.
Not meal-related or positional here, making reflux a poorer fit.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The key move is recognising that new-onset crescendo effort pain is UNSTABLE angina, not stable angina — this changes the disposition from routine to urgent.

differential.diagnosis: Unstable angina / acute coronary syndrome spectrum
differential.evidence_basis: Crescendo exertional ischaemic pain with risk factors — cannot exclude evolving infarction without ECG and troponin.
Same substrate; distinguished only by troponin — must be actively excluded, not assumed away.
Can cause exertional chest pain; would classically have an ejection systolic murmur radiating to the carotids (a clinician-only finding, absent here on exam).
Low probability given the exertional-relieved-by-rest pattern, but a can't-miss cause of chest pain to keep on the radar if the picture shifts to tearing/rest pain.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage the disposition is driven by 'cannot exclude ACS remotely', not by pinning the exact ACS subtype — that requires ECG/troponin in person.

differential.diagnosis: Ischaemic heart disease — new-onset crescendo/unstable angina, ACS spectrum
differential.evidence_basis: Classic ischaemic history + risk factors + accelerating course; final risk stratification (unstable angina vs NSTEMI vs stable) needs in-person ECG and serial troponin.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth-correct endpoint is 'suspected ACS — urgent same-day in-person assessment', NOT a confirmed benign angina label.

red_flags.description: New-onset, first-ever exertional chest pain that is accelerating (crescendo) over 48 hours — the hallmark of unstable angina
red_flags.clinical_significance: Crescendo new-onset angina is unstable and carries a substantial short-term risk of myocardial infarction; it mandates urgent in-person work-up, not routine review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Labelling it 'stable angina' or a benign mimic and deferring to routine care risks an unheralded MI or cardiac arrest — especially in a patient living alone.

red_flags.description: Exertional central chest tightness radiating to the jaw and left arm with nausea and breathlessness
red_flags.clinical_significance: A high-specificity ischaemic constellation that markedly raises the probability of coronary disease.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to weight the radiation and associated features risks anchoring on reflux/musculoskeletal pain and under-triage.

red_flags.description: Multiple modifiable cardiovascular risk factors — current smoking and hyperlipidaemia with poor statin adherence
red_flags.clinical_significance: Raises pre-test probability of coronary disease and worsens prognosis; reinforces that this pain must be treated as cardiac until excluded.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Discounting risk factors contributes to false reassurance.

red_flags.description: S4 gallop audible at the apex on auscultation (clinician-only finding)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Reflects reduced left-ventricular compliance from ischaemia, supporting a cardiac cause; obtainable only by a physically-present clinician.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not obtainable via telehealth — the AI Doctor must not rely on or claim auscultatory findings.

red_flags.description: Lives alone — no bystander to summon help if a severe ischaemic event occurs
red_flags.clinical_significance: Social isolation raises the risk of a fatal outcome from an unwitnessed event and lowers the threshold for urgent, supervised assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ignoring the isolation may lead to an unsafe 'wait and see at home' plan.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Recognising the pain as angina but calling it 'stable' and deferring to routine care — missing that new-onset crescendo effort pain is UNSTABLE and ACS-spectrum.
Latching onto reflux or musculoskeletal chest wall pain because they are common and reassuring, despite an exertional, radiating, risk-factor-laden pattern.
Being reassured because the patient is currently comfortable at rest — angina is intermittent and a pain-free interval does not exclude an evolving ACS.
Trying to 'confirm' or exclude cardiac ischaemia remotely; the diagnosis needs a 12-lead ECG and troponin that telehealth cannot provide, so the correct output is escalation to in-person assessment.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Cannot be completed via telehealth — the ECG and troponin components are unavailable; history and risk factors alone place this at moderate-to-high concern
risk_scores.clinical_implication: An incomplete HEART score must not be used to reassure; the missing ECG/troponin are exactly why in-person assessment is required.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Resting 12-lead ECG (ischaemic changes / dynamic ST-T changes)
Blood pressure and heart rate without a validated home device
Cardiac auscultation (S4 gallop, murmurs) and JVP/oedema assessment
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (emergency department or equivalent acute service) for a resting 12-lead ECG and serial troponin to exclude an evolving MI; advise immediate 000 if chest pain occurs at rest, lasts more than 15 minutes, or is not relieved after up to 3 doses of GTN, or is accompanied by sweating, severe breathlessness, or feeling faint.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High risk of premature closure toward 'stable angina' or a benign mimic; the AI Doctor cannot exclude ACS remotely and must escalate rather than reassure.

guidelines_referenced.reference: National Heart Foundation of Australia / CSANZ ACS Guidelines 2016
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Suspected acute coronary syndrome (including new-onset or accelerating angina) requires prompt ECG and troponin assessment; aspirin should be given early unless contraindicated.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE CG95 — Chest pain of recent onset
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Recent-onset exertional chest pain suspected to be cardiac warrants urgent assessment; treat as unstable angina/ACS if pain is new, at rest, or of increasing frequency.
